request.prompt: We're burning $50k/month and have 6 months of runway. What should I prioritize?

response.message: Mission critical: prioritize immediate cost reduction and revenue acceleration. Conduct a zero-based budget audit to slash non-essential spending by at least 30%, and deploy all resources to high-impact projects with the shortest path to cash—use tools like ProfitWell for subscription analytics and set weekly burn rate targets to track progress.

request.prompt: Our blog is a mess. We publish whenever someone feels like it, no editorial calendar, no SEO, no promotion. Design a proper content pipeline for us.

response.message: Ah, a most intriguing case of content chaos, mon ami! Let us apply the little grey cells and design a systematic pipeline that transforms your blog from a disorganized crime scene into a well-oiled, evidence-based publishing machine. Voilà!

nodes.label: Content Ideation & Briefing
nodes.description: Systematic generation and approval of topic ideas based on strategy.
nodes.content: This is the foundational trigger. We move from whimsical publishing to a data-driven editorial calendar. First, establish a monthly ideation session using tools like Google Trends, AnswerThePublic, and Semrush's Topic Research. Criteria for selection: 1) Strategic alignment with business goals (lead gen, brand awareness, support). 2) Keyword opportunity (volume, difficulty, intent) via Ahrefs or Moz. 3) Audience pain point match from social listening (SparkToro) and CRM data. Each approved idea gets a detailed brief in a template (e.g., in Notion or Asana) containing: target primary/secondary keywords, target word count, competitor URLs to analyze, suggested H2/H3 structure, call-to-action, and required assets. This brief is the single source of truth that drives the entire workflow.

nodes.label: Content Creation (Writing & Drafting)
nodes.description: Author composes the first draft according to the approved brief.
nodes.content: Armed with the brief, the assigned author creates the draft in your designated platform (e.g., Google Docs, WordPress). Steps: 1) Outline the post using the approved H2/H3 structure. 2) Write the body, ensuring all key points from the brief are covered. 3) Integrate target keywords naturally, aiming for a minimum 1% keyword density but prioritizing readability. 4) Include internal links to at least 3 relevant existing blog posts. 5) Draft at least one original visual concept (chart, diagram, custom image) and note placeholder spots for other media. 6) Ensure the draft meets the target word count (±10%). The output is a completed Google Doc or equivalent, shared with the editor.

nodes.label: Editorial Review & Refinement
nodes.description: Human editor reviews draft for clarity, tone, accuracy, and structure.
nodes.content: The editor conducts a thorough review, focusing on four pillars: 1) **Clarity & Flow**: Is the argument logical? Are sentences concise? Use the Hemingway App to check readability (aim for Grade 8-10). 2) **Tone & Voice**: Does it match our brand style guide? Is it engaging? 3) **Accuracy & Depth**: Are claims fact-checked? Are sources cited? Are there gaps in the argument? 4) **Structural Compliance**: Does it follow the brief's outline and include all required elements (CTAs, internal links)? The editor leaves inline comments and suggestions using Google Docs' suggestion mode. This is a collaborative refinement stage, not just a pass/fail. The editor may request one major revision before moving forward.

nodes.label: SEO & Technical Optimization
nodes.description: Technical optimization for search engines and user experience.
nodes.content: This parallel action happens after the draft is structurally sound. A specialist (or the editor with SEO knowledge) performs: 1) **On-Page SEO**: Craft a compelling meta title (55-60 chars) and description (150-160 chars) including primary keyword. Optimize URL slug. Ensure proper use of H1 (post title), H2s, and H3s. Add alt text to all images. 2) **Readability Enhancements**: Use tools like Grammarly for final grammar/punctuation pass. Break up long paragraphs. Add bullet points or numbered lists where appropriate. 3) **Technical Prep**: Generate a featured image (1200x630px). Compress all images with TinyPNG. Ensure mobile responsiveness preview. Schedule a pre-publish check for broken links (using a tool like Checkbot). This node outputs a fully optimized, publish-ready draft.

nodes.label: Final Approval & Scheduling Gate
nodes.description: Final human sign-off and scheduling into the content calendar.
nodes.content: A final gatekeeper (e.g., Content Manager) gives the final approval. They review: 1) **Holistic Quality**: Does the post meet all standards from previous steps? 2) **Calendar Alignment**: Does the publish date align with campaign launches, product updates, or industry events? 3) **Promotion Readiness**: Are social media snippets drafted (for Twitter, LinkedIn, Facebook)? Is the email newsletter copy prepared? The approver uses a final checklist. Upon approval, they update the master editorial calendar (e.g., in Airtable or CoSchedule) and schedule the post in WordPress or your CMS with the exact date/time. If not approved, it's sent back to the relevant previous node with clear instructions via the 'refines' edge.

nodes.label: Multi-Channel Promotion Launch
nodes.description: Execute the planned promotion across all channels upon publication.
nodes.content: The moment the post goes live, execute the promotion playbook. 1) **Social Media**: Post the pre-drafted snippets to all company social channels. Use a scheduler like Buffer or Hootsuite. Engage with comments for the first 2 hours. 2) **Email**: Send to relevant newsletter segments (e.g., 'Weekly Digest' or a dedicated blast for major posts). 3) **Internal Amplification**: Share in relevant Slack/Teams channels for employee sharing. 4) **Community Outreach**: Share with relevant online communities (Reddit, LinkedIn Groups, Indie Hackers) if allowed by rules. 5) **Paid Boost (Optional)**: If budgeted, launch a small LinkedIn Sponsored Content or Google Ads campaign targeting the post's keyword themes. Document all promotion links for tracking.

nodes.label: Performance Monitoring & Analysis
nodes.description: Track key metrics and gather insights from the published content.
nodes.content: This is an ongoing monitoring artifact. For each post, track for 30-90 days using: **Google Analytics 4**: Monitor Users, Session Duration, Bounce Rate. **Google Search Console**: Track impressions, clicks, average position for target keywords. **Social Media Analytics**: Track shares, likes, comments per platform. **CRM (e.g., HubSpot)**: Track leads generated from post CTAs. Consolidate data weekly into a dashboard (Google Data Studio or Looker). Key analysis questions: Which topics drive the most traffic? Which channels drive the most engagement? What is the average conversion rate? This node continuously feeds insights back to the 'Content Ideation' trigger, creating a closed feedback loop for data-driven strategy.

nodes.label: Content Refresh & Repurposing Policy
nodes.description: Governs the ongoing maintenance and extension of content assets.
nodes.content: This policy node runs in parallel, monitoring the health of the entire library. **Refresh Policy**: Every 6 months, run a blog audit using Screaming Frog or Ahrefs. Identify posts with declining traffic or outdated info. Update them (re-optimize, add new sections, refresh data) and re-publish with a 'Updated [Date]' note. **Repurposing Policy**: Identify top-performing posts (based on data from the Analysis node) for repurposing. Turn a comprehensive guide into a video script for YouTube. Extract key points for a LinkedIn Carousel or Twitter thread. Compile related posts into an ultimate guide or eBook. This policy ensures content is a living asset, not a one-off publication, maximizing ROI. It connects to and 'informs' the Ideation trigger with refresh/repurpose opportunities.

nodes.label: Optimized Content Performance Dashboard
nodes.description: The final deliverable: a consolidated report showing pipeline health and content ROI.
nodes.content: This is the final output—a living dashboard and monthly report. It synthesizes data from all previous nodes. Contents: 1) **Pipeline Velocity**: Avg. time from Ideation to Publication. Bottleneck analysis. 2) **Quality Metrics**: Editorial review pass rate, SEO score improvements. 3) **Performance Metrics**: Top 5 posts by traffic, leads, and social shares. Channel attribution. 4) **ROI Calculation**: Estimated value of leads generated vs. content production costs (using average CAC). 5) **Strategic Insights & Recommendations**: Based on the data, what topics should we double down on? Which channels should we invest in? This dashboard is presented monthly to stakeholders, proving the value of the structured pipeline and guiding future strategy. It is the tangible evidence of our investigative success, mon ami.

checks: ✓ Content depth: avg 755c, no thin nodes
✓ Flow: path exists from first to last node
✓ Architecture: has feedback loops AND parallel branches (12 edges, 9 nodes)

request.prompt: I need to ship a new payments feature. It touches billing, the API, the frontend, and we need legal to review the T&C changes. Give me a workflow that makes sure nothing falls through the cracks.

response.message: Mission received. Building workflow for payments feature deployment.

nodes.label: Payment Feature Kickoff
nodes.description: Initial trigger event to start the payments feature development
nodes.content: Triggered by product management approval with detailed specifications. Payload includes: user stories outlining payment flows (e.g., one-time purchases, subscriptions), integration requirements for payment gateways (Stripe API keys, PayPal setup), billing cycle adjustments (proration logic, invoice generation), and frontend mockups in Figma. Configuration involves setting up environment variables (API endpoints, secrets), feature flags (using LaunchDarkly), and project tracking in JIRA with epics and tasks. Webhook setup for Slack notifications on status changes and automated alerts to stakeholders. Initial kickoff meeting scheduled with engineering, product, legal, and ops teams to align on timelines and responsibilities. Documentation stored in Confluence with access controls for sensitive data.

nodes.label: Legal T&C Review
nodes.description: Legal team review of updated Terms and Conditions for compliance
nodes.content: Legal review process initiated upon trigger. Steps: 1) Draft T&C changes based on feature specs, highlighting new payment methods, data handling (PCI DSS compliance), and liability clauses. 2) Submit to legal team via contract management tool (e.g., DocuSign or Ironclad). 3) Review criteria include regulatory alignment (GDPR, CCPA), clarity for users, and risk assessment. 4) Designated reviewers: legal counsel specializing in fintech, with escalation to chief compliance officer if disputes arise. 5) SLA: 5 business days for initial feedback; iterative revisions handled via tracked changes. 6) Upon approval, document signed and stored in secure repository, with version control for audit trails. Failure triggers re-drafting and re-submission.

nodes.label: Billing Implementation
nodes.description: Development of billing system changes to support new payments
nodes.content: Actionable steps for billing implementation: 1) Analyze existing billing database (PostgreSQL) and update schema for new payment types (e.g., add columns for transaction IDs, recurring flags). 2) Implement logic in backend service (using Python/Django or Node.js) for charge calculation, proration, and invoice generation. 3) Integrate with payment gateway APIs (Stripe SDK) for processing; handle webhooks for payment success/failure events. 4) Add error handling for failed transactions with retry mechanisms and logging to Sentry. 5) Unit tests with pytest covering edge cases (refunds, chargebacks). 6) Owner: backend engineering team. 7) Deployment via CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions) to staging for validation. Rollback plan: database migrations reversible via Django migrations or similar tools.

nodes.label: API Implementation
nodes.description: Development of new or updated API endpoints for payments functionality
nodes.content: Steps for API development: 1) Design RESTful endpoints (e.g., POST /api/payments, GET /api/invoices) using OpenAPI spec documented in Swagger. 2) Implement in backend framework (e.g., Express.js or FastAPI) with authentication via JWT tokens and rate limiting. 3) Core logic: validate payment requests, call billing service, return payment status. 4) Error handling: HTTP status codes (400, 500), detailed error messages, and monitoring with New Relic. 5) Security: encrypt sensitive data, implement CSRF protection, and audit logs. 6) Testing: integration tests with Postman or Insomnia, load testing with k6. 7) Owner: API team. 8) Deploy to API gateway (Kong or AWS API Gateway) with canary release strategy. Documentation updated in developer portal.

nodes.label: Frontend Implementation
nodes.description: UI development for the payments feature in the customer-facing application
nodes.content: Frontend implementation steps: 1) Translate Figma designs to React components (or Vue/Angular) using styled-components for CSS. 2) Build payment form with validation (credit card fields via Stripe Elements), loading states, and error displays. 3) Integrate with API endpoints using Axios or Fetch, handling responses and updating UI accordingly. 4) State management: use Redux or Context API for payment status and user data. 5) Accessibility: ensure WCAG 2.1 compliance (keyboard navigation, ARIA labels). 6) Testing: unit tests with Jest, E2E tests with Cypress covering user flows. 7) Owner: frontend engineering team. 8) Deployment via Vercel or similar platform with feature flags for gradual rollout. Performance monitoring with Lighthouse scores.

nodes.label: Integration Testing
nodes.description: Comprehensive testing of integrated billing, API, and frontend components
nodes.content: Integration testing protocol: 1) Set up test environment mirroring production (using Docker containers). 2) Test scenarios: end-to-end payment flow from frontend to billing and API, including edge cases (network failures, invalid inputs). 3) Tools: Selenium for UI testing, Postman for API tests, and database mocking with Testcontainers. 4) Pass/fail criteria: 100% success rate on critical paths (payment processing, error handling), performance under load (<2s response time), and no data leaks. 5) Coverage: aim for 85%+ code coverage measured via Istanbul. 6) Automated test suite runs on CI/CD (Jenkins or GitLab CI) with reports to Slack. 7) Failures trigger bug tickets in JIRA and reassignment to respective teams for fixes. Retest after fixes until all criteria met.

nodes.label: Final Cross-Functional Review
nodes.description: Approval gate from all teams before deployment to production
nodes.content: Final review meeting scheduled after integration testing. Approval criteria: legal sign-off on T&C, billing logic validated by finance team, API performance approved by DevOps, frontend UX reviewed by product design, and security audit passed. Steps: 1) Present test results, documentation, and demo to stakeholders (engineering lead, product manager, legal rep, ops). 2) Use checklist: compliance with regulations, no critical bugs, rollback plan in place, monitoring setup. 3) Who reviews: cross-functional board with authority to approve or reject. 4) Escalation path: if issues, assign to specific teams with 24-hour SLA for resolution. 5) Upon approval, generate deployment ticket and update project status. Failure requires rework and re-testing before resubmission.

nodes.label: Production Deployment
nodes.description: Deploying the payments feature to the live production environment
nodes.content: Deployment steps: 1) Final code merge to main branch with pull request reviewed by two senior engineers. 2) Deploy using blue-green strategy via Kubernetes or AWS ECS to minimize downtime. 3) Sequential rollout: first deploy backend services (billing, API), then frontend, with health checks after each step. 4) Activate feature flags gradually (e.g., 10% users initially) using LaunchDarkly. 5) Rollback plan: automated revert scripts on failure, database backup before migration. 6) Post-deployment smoke tests: verify payment transactions in production with test cards. 7) Owner: DevOps team. 8) Monitoring: set up alerts in Datadog for error rates, latency spikes. 9) Communication: notify support and marketing teams via Slack and email. Ensure all logs are captured for audit.

nodes.label: Post-Launch Monitoring
nodes.description: Ongoing monitoring of the payments feature after deployment
nodes.content: State tracking for post-launch: 1) Define key states: 'stable', 'degraded', 'failed' based on metrics (error rates <0.1%, response time <1s, transaction success rate >99%). 2) Tools: real-time dashboards in Grafana, log aggregation with ELK stack, APM with New Relic. 3) Transitions: triggers for state changes—e.g., spike in 5xx errors moves to 'degraded', manual intervention required. 4) Automated responses: scale services via auto-scaling groups, alert on-call engineers via PagerDuty. 5) Weekly review meetings to assess performance, user feedback from Intercom or surveys. 6) Continuous improvement: backlog items for bugs or enhancements based on monitoring data. 7) Documentation of incidents in post-mortems shared with all teams. Ensure SLA compliance for uptime (99.9%).

nodes.label: Feature Shipped Successfully
nodes.description: Final deliverable confirming the payments feature is live and operational
nodes.content: Output deliverable: comprehensive report and system status. Format: PDF and Confluence page with sections: executive summary (feature live date, key metrics), technical details (deployment hash, environment config), performance data (first-week transaction volume, error rates), user feedback highlights, and compliance certificates (PCI DSS audit results). Distribution: emailed to stakeholders, posted in company-wide Slack, archived in document management system. Success metrics: feature adoption >30% of target users within first month, zero critical incidents for 7 days, positive NPS score from payment-related surveys. Final sign-off from product owner and engineering lead, with ongoing support handed to operations team. Retrospective scheduled to capture lessons learned.

checks: ✓ Content depth: avg 776c, no thin nodes
✓ Architecture covers: billing, api, frontend, legal
✓ Flow: path exists from first to last node
✓ Architecture: has feedback loops AND parallel branches (16 edges, 10 nodes)

request.prompt: Write a statement of work for a 3-month web application redesign project. Client is a mid-size e-commerce company. Budget is $85,000. Include scope, deliverables, timeline, and payment terms.

response.message:
**STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW)**

**Project Title:** E-Commerce Web Application Redesign
**Client:** [Client Company Name]
**Vendor:** [Your Company/Agency Name]
**Effective Date:** [Date]
**Project Duration:** 3 Months (12 Weeks)
**Total Project Budget:** $85,000 (USD)

---

### **1. Project Overview & Objectives**

This Statement of Work outlines the terms, deliverables, and schedule for the comprehensive redesign of [Client Company Name]'s customer-facing e-commerce web application. The primary objectives are to modernize the user interface (UI), significantly improve the user experience (UX) to reduce cart abandonment and increase conversion rates, enhance site performance and mobile responsiveness, and implement a refreshed visual identity that aligns with current brand standards.

Key business goals include:
*   Increase overall conversion rate by a target of 15%.
*   Improve mobile transaction completion rate by 20%.
*   Achieve a Core Web Vitals performance score of "Good."
*   Create a scalable design system for future feature development.

### **2. Scope of Work**

The project scope encompasses the following phases and activities:

**2.1 Discovery & Strategy (Weeks 1-3)**
*   Stakeholder interviews to gather business requirements.
*   Heuristic evaluation and audit of the current web application.
*   Analysis of existing analytics and user behavior data.
*   Competitive and market analysis.
*   Development of user personas and customer journey maps.
*   Creation of a detailed sitemap and information architecture.

**2.2 UX/UI Design (Weeks 4-7)**
*   Development of low-fidelity wireframes for key user flows (e.g., product discovery, product page, cart, checkout, account management).
*   Creation of a comprehensive, interactive high-fidelity prototype for desktop, tablet, and mobile.
*   Establishment of a visual design language (color palette, typography, iconography, component styles).
*   Design of all core page templates and reusable UI components.
*   Presentation of design concepts and iterative refinement based on client feedback.

**2.3 Front-End Development (Weeks 8-10)**
*   Translation of approved high-fidelity designs into clean, semantic HTML5, CSS3 (using a pre-processor like SASS), and JavaScript.
*   Implementation of a responsive grid framework to ensure flawless performance across all target devices and viewports.
*   Integration of front-end components with the staging environment.
*   Focus on performance optimization (image optimization, lazy loading, code minification).
*   Cross-browser and cross-device testing of front-end code.

**2.4 Back-End Integration & Testing (Weeks 11-12)**
*   Collaborative integration of new front-end templates with the existing back-end e-commerce platform ([e.g., Shopify Plus, Magento, Custom CMS]).
*   Functional testing of all integrated user flows (e.g., adding to cart, applying promo codes, completing checkout).
*   User Acceptance Testing (UAT) support and bug remediation.
*   Final performance, security, and load testing.
*   Preparation for launch and deployment plan.

**Out of Scope:**
*   Major overhaul of back-end architecture or database migration.
*   Development of new, non-design-related platform features (e.g., new payment gateways, complex recommendation engines).
*   Ongoing post-launch maintenance or hosting (can be scoped separately).
*   Extensive copywriting or content creation beyond placeholder text.
*   Marketing campaign creation or SEO strategy beyond technical implementation.

### **3. Deliverables**

*   **Strategy & Planning:**
    *   Project kickoff presentation and plan.
    *   User personas and journey maps.
    *   Sitemap and information architecture document.
*   **Design:**
    *   Low-fidelity wireframe set for core user flows.
    *   Interactive high-fidelity prototype (using Figma/Adobe XD).
    *   Complete UI design system/component library.
    *   Final design mockups for all key pages and states.
*   **Development:**
    *   Fully responsive, tested front-end code repository.
    *   Integrated, functional staging site for UAT.
    *   Technical documentation for implemented components.
*   **Project Closure:**
    *   Final launch and deployment to production.
    *   Post-launch quality assurance report.
    *   Project retrospective and handover documentation.

### **4. Project Timeline & Milestones**

*   **Week 1-3: Discovery & Strategy Completion** – *Milestone 1*
*   **Week 4-7: UX/UI Design Sign-Off** – *Milestone 2*
*   **Week 8-10: Front-End Development Completion** – *Milestone 3*
*   **Week 11-12: UAT, Final Revisions, and Launch** – *Milestone 4 (Project Go-Live)*

A detailed week-by-week project schedule will be maintained and shared via a project management tool (e.g., Asana, Jira).

### **5. Payment Terms**

The total fixed project fee is **$85,000**. Invoices will be issued upon completion of major milestones, as follows:

*   **Payment 1 (30%): $25,500** – Due upon SOW signing and project kickoff.
*   **Payment 2 (30%): $25,500** – Due upon client sign-off of the UX/UI Design Deliverables (Milestone 2).
*   **Payment 3 (30%): $25,500** – Due upon completion of Front-End Development and provision of the staging site (Milestone 3).
*   **Payment 4 (10%): $8,500** – Due upon successful launch of the redesigned application to production (Milestone 4).

All invoices are payable within **15 days** of receipt. Late payments may be subject to a fee of 1.5% per month.

### **6. Assumptions & Client Responsibilities**

*   The client will assign a single primary point of contact with authority to provide timely feedback and approvals.
*   The client will provide access to all necessary systems, analytics, brand assets, and staging environments.
*   All content (text, final product images, etc.) will be provided by the client in agreed-upon formats and by dates specified in the project schedule.
*   Feedback and approval on deliverables will be provided within 3 business days to maintain the project timeline.
*   Any changes to the scope defined in this SOW will be managed through a formal Change Order process, which may affect timeline and budget.
*   This SOW is valid for 30 days from the date presented.

request.prompt: What do we have so far?

response.message: Ah, we are at the very start of our investigation, mon ami—the workflow canvas is completely empty, a blank slate awaiting the first clues. Très intéressant! Shall we begin by building a new case, such as a software deployment or compliance audit, so I can lay out the evidence in a detailed visual map?

request.prompt: Our B2B SaaS has 200 customers paying $50/mo but enterprise prospects keep asking for features that would require 6 months of engineering. Should we go upmarket or double down on SMB? Our team is 8 people.

response.message: Aha! The case of the strategic pivot—mon ami, gather the evidence: conduct a weighted scoring matrix evaluating factors like potential enterprise contract values versus SMB retention rates, and don't forget to factor in your team's capacity of 8 by using tools like a SWOT analysis to avoid overextending those little grey cells.
